explicit_text: On September 11th, I was in the office when I first heard that a plane had hit the World Trade Center towers. The situation quickly escalated as the second tower was hit, and then the Pentagon was attacked, leading to the evacuation of the White House and the shutdown of the District of Columbia. It was a chaotic day with communication systems overwhelmed and roads blocked, but we stayed in the building to manage emergency operations and maintain contact with our teams. The attacks deeply impacted how we viewed emergency preparedness and continuity planning.

explicit_text: The District of Columbia was essentially shut down on September 11th, with an evacuation order for all facilities and people to go home. This caused massive traffic jams as nearly a million people tried to leave at once, with key bridges like the 14th Street Bridge closed due to suspicious packages. Communication systems were overwhelmed, and the city was in a state of lockdown, which made managing the emergency response extremely challenging.
